l1: Question 1 — When did the ReFS corruption issue first appear?
Question 2 — Is the corruption isolated to one volume or multiple?
Question 3 — Are users reporting file access errors or application crashes?
Question 4 — Has the server experienced unexpected shutdowns or power loss?
Question 5 — Was the volume recently resized, migrated, or restored?
Question 6 — Are permissions or ACLs failing on affected files?
Question 7 — Is the issue impacting production workloads or test environments?
Question 8 — Are shadow copies or checkpoints failing on the volume?
l2: Advanced question 1 — Are there Event Viewer logs showing ReFS errors (Event ID 100, 101)?
Advanced question 2 — Is the corruption linked to underlying disk or RAID issues?
Advanced question 3 — Is deduplication enabled on the ReFS volume?
Advanced question 4 — Is the volume part of clustered storage or Storage Spaces Direct?
Advanced question 5 — Are there metadata integrity errors reported by ReFS?
Advanced question 6 — Has BitLocker or encryption been applied?
Advanced question 7 — Is corruption reproducible after restoring from backup?
Advanced question 8 — Are applications reporting ReFS metadata errors?

l1: Command 1 — Run PowerShell Get-Volume to confirm health status
Command 2 — Check Event Viewer → System log for ReFS errors
Command 3 — Run Get-FileIntegrity to check ReFS metadata integrity
Command 4 — Verify disk health with Get-Disk
Command 5 — Attempt file access to confirm corruption symptoms
Command 6 — Review Disk Management for volume state
Command 7 — Run fsutil fsinfo volumeinfo to check filesystem details
Command 8 — Check permissions with icacls on affected files
l2: Advanced command 1 — Run Repair-Volume -DriveLetter X -OfflineScanAndFix for ReFS volumes
Advanced command 2 — Query cluster logs if volume is clustered
Advanced command 3 — Run Get-StoragePool and Get-VirtualDisk for underlying storage health
Advanced command 4 — Use Get-FileIntegrity -FileName <path> for specific file checks
Advanced command 5 — Review deduplication logs if enabled
Advanced command 6 — Run DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth if OS corruption suspected
Advanced command 7 — Use Performance Monitor counters for ReFS metadata operations
Advanced command 8 — Run Get-PhysicalDisk to confirm SMART health

l1: Step 1 — Restart the server and rerun Repair-Volume
Step 2 — Restore affected files from backup
Step 3 — Verify and reset file permissions with icacls
Step 4 — Ensure sufficient free disk space for repair operations
Step 5 — Disable deduplication temporarily if causing corruption
Step 6 — Validate disk health with vendor diagnostic tools
Step 7 — Run Get-FileIntegrity to confirm metadata integrity
Step 8 — Check cluster resource health if clustered
l2: Advanced fix 1 — Run Repair-Volume -OfflineScanAndFix for deeper repair
Advanced fix 2 — Rebuild RAID array if underlying disk issues detected
Advanced fix 3 — Restore volume from snapshot or checkpoint
Advanced fix 4 — Recreate corrupted ReFS volume and restore data from backup
Advanced fix 5 — Update storage controller firmware and drivers
Advanced fix 6 — Escalate to Microsoft support if ReFS metadata corruption persists
Advanced fix 7 — Perform cluster validation if clustered storage is impacted
Advanced fix 8 — Reconfigure deduplication or disable if unstable

l1: Collection 1 — Export Event Viewer logs from System → ReFS errors
Collection 2 — Run Get-Volume and export results
Collection 3 — Execute Repair-Volume output and save
Collection 4 — Capture screenshot of Disk Management showing affected volumes
Collection 5 — Collect OS build and patch level
Collection 6 — Export fsutil fsinfo volumeinfo output
Collection 7 — Gather icacls permissions output for affected files
Collection 8 — Save SMART data from Get-PhysicalDisk
l2: Advanced collection 1 — Export Repair-Volume -OfflineScanAndFix detailed logs
Advanced collection 2 — Collect Get-FileIntegrity results for metadata validation
Advanced collection 3 — Gather cluster logs if volume is clustered
Advanced collection 4 — Collect RAID controller logs and config dump
Advanced collection 5 — Export deduplication logs if enabled
Advanced collection 6 — Gather DISM logs if OS corruption suspected
Advanced collection 7 — Collect firmware/driver versions for storage controllers
Advanced collection 8 — Export Performance Monitor counters for ReFS operations

steps.expect: Volume shows degraded or unhealthy state

steps.action: Restore files from backup if unrecoverable
